title: Rosuvastatin vs Atorvastatin for Diabetic Macular Edema — Asking the Wrong Question
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: why no statin molecule is proven superior for DME, why the real adjunct is fenofibrate (ACCORD Eye / FIELD) yet its retinopathy benefit is largely lipid-independent, the renal/drug-interaction caveats, and why lipids are adjunctive to anti-VEGF/laser and glycaemic-BP control
authors: Resident/fellowship training module — illustrative teaching case

relatedChapters.title: Complex Diabetes Management in Advanced CKD
Pattern B LDL: Small, Dense LDL
Primary-Prevention Lipids & Aspirin in an Indian Diabetic

patient.chiefComplaint: Worsening macular hard exudates and centre-involving edema on diabetic eye review.
pmh: Type 2 diabetes with non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy and diabetic macular edema
Diabetic dyslipidaemia (raised triglycerides, prominent macular hard exudates)

caseDescription: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, non-proliferative retinopathy and centre-involving diabetic macular edema (DME) with prominent macular hard exudates is on atorvastatin 20 mg. His LDL-C is near target but triglycerides are 220 mg/dL. The referring team asks whether switching atorvastatin to rosuvastatin (more potent, milligram-for-milligram) would improve the macular edema and clear the hard exudates, given debates about statin lipophilicity and intraretinal 'pleiotropic' effects. The teaching task is to show that this is the wrong lever to pull, and to lay out what actually changes retinopathy outcomes.
keyFindings: No head-to-head evidence that rosuvastatin beats atorvastatin (or vice versa) for DME or hard exudates
Statin lipophilicity (atorvastatin) vs hydrophilicity (rosuvastatin) is a mechanistic hypothesis that has not translated into a clinical DME advantage
The evidence-based lipid adjunct for diabetic retinopathy is fenofibrate added to a statin (ACCORD Eye, FIELD) — not a statin swap
Crucially, fenofibrate's retinopathy benefit appears largely INDEPENDENT of baseline lipids/triglyceride lowering — so 'it just lowers triglycerides' is an over-simplification
Fenofibrate has real caveats: reversible creatinine rise / renal dosing, avoid gemfibrozil with statins (myopathy), and retinopathy is an off-label/region-dependent indication
Lipid therapy is adjunctive: vision-threatening DME is treated primarily with anti-VEGF or focal laser, on a foundation of glycaemic and blood-pressure control
finalDiagnosis: Diabetic macular edema with hard exudates in a patient with diabetic dyslipidaemia, where the proposed intervention (switching atorvastatin to rosuvastatin to treat the eye) is not supported by evidence. The correct framework: keep the patient on an effective statin titrated to the systemic LDL-C/ApoB target (choosing the molecule on potency, tolerability and drug interactions, not on the eye); consider adding fenofibrate as the evidence-based adjunct to slow retinopathy progression and reduce first laser need (ACCORD Eye, FIELD), recognising that the benefit is largely lipid-independent and that renal function and fibrate–statin interactions must be checked; and remember that lipid management is adjunctive — sight-threatening/centre-involving DME is managed primarily by ophthalmology with anti-VEGF or focal laser, built on tight glycaemic and blood-pressure control.
learningObjectives: Explain why there is no proven superiority of one statin molecule over another for diabetic macular edema, and what should actually drive statin choice.
Cite ACCORD Eye and FIELD for fenofibrate's effect on retinopathy progression and laser need, and note the surprising lipid-independence of that benefit.
List the practical fenofibrate caveats: renal dosing and reversible creatinine rise, avoiding gemfibrozil with statins, and the off-label/region-dependent retinopathy indication.
Place lipid therapy correctly as an adjunct to anti-VEGF/focal laser and to glycaemic and blood-pressure control for DME.
Build a stepwise lipid-management framework for diabetic dyslipidaemia with retinopathy.

items.title: Why 'rosuvastatin vs atorvastatin for the eye' is the wrong question
items.content: There is no high-quality head-to-head evidence that either rosuvastatin or atorvastatin is superior for diabetic macular edema or for clearing hard exudates, so switching molecules to treat the retina is not justified. Statins as a class plausibly help retinopathy through systemic LDL-C/ApoB lowering and anti-inflammatory (pleiotropic) effects that stabilise the blood–retinal barrier, but that benefit is not molecule-specific. The right driver for choosing between the two is the systemic agenda: which agent gets this patient to the LDL-C/ApoB target with the best tolerability and fewest drug interactions. Framed correctly, the eye does not pick the statin — the lipid target and the patient's drug list do.
keyPoints: No head-to-head data favour one statin for DME/hard exudates
Any statin benefit is class-level (systemic LDL/ApoB + anti-inflammatory), not molecule-specific
Choose the statin on lipid target, tolerability and interactions — not on the retina

items.title: Why the lipophilicity argument is a hypothesis, not a clinical rule
items.content: Atorvastatin is lipophilic and rosuvastatin is hydrophilic, and lipophilic statins cross cell membranes more readily — which spawned attractive hypotheses about distinct intraretinal pleiotropic actions (downregulating VEGF, reducing NADPH-oxidase oxidative stress, improving retinal endothelial function). These are mechanistically interesting but remain unproven at the bedside: the physicochemical difference has not translated into a demonstrated DME advantage in clinical studies. Rosuvastatin's greater milligram-for-milligram potency delivers an equivalent systemic anti-inflammatory and barrier-stabilising effect by driving LDL-C/ApoB lower. The teaching point is to treat 'lipophilic statins are better for the retina' as a doubt-flagged hypothesis, not a prescribing instruction.
keyPoints: Lipophilic (atorvastatin) vs hydrophilic (rosuvastatin) underlies the pleiotropy hypothesis
The hypothesised intraretinal advantage is unproven clinically
Don't switch statins on physicochemistry alone — flag it as hypothesis

items.title: How to set the statin target in diabetic dyslipidaemia
items.content: Drive the statin by cardiovascular risk, not the fundus. Most patients with type 2 diabetes are high or very-high cardiovascular risk; guideline targets (e.g. ESC/EAS) are an LDL-C below ~70 mg/dL for high risk and below ~55 mg/dL with at least a 50% reduction for very-high risk (established ASCVD or diabetes with target-organ damage/multiple risk factors). Use high-intensity dosing to get there — atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg — and add ezetimibe (and, if needed, a PCSK9 inhibitor) when the statin alone misses target. ApoB is a useful secondary target in diabetic dyslipidaemia because it captures the full burden of atherogenic particles, which standard LDL-C can underestimate when triglycerides are high. Hitting this systemic target is the main job; the retinal question is separate.
keyPoints: Target by CV risk: LDL <70 (high) or <55 with ≥50% reduction (very-high)
High-intensity statin ± ezetimibe ± PCSK9i to reach target
ApoB is a useful secondary target when triglycerides are raised

items.title: Why fenofibrate — not a statin swap — is the evidence-based retinopathy adjunct
items.content: Two landmark trials put fenofibrate, added to background therapy, on the retinopathy map. In FIELD (type 2 diabetes), fenofibrate reduced the need for first laser treatment for retinopathy and slowed progression in those with pre-existing retinopathy. In ACCORD Eye, fenofibrate added to simvastatin produced roughly a 40% relative reduction in retinopathy progression over four years. So when the clinical goal is to pharmacologically slow lipid-associated retinal disease, the high-yield move is adding a fibrate, not exchanging one statin for another. This is the single most important correction to the 'rosuvastatin vs atorvastatin' framing: the meaningful lever in the trials was fenofibrate, used alongside a statin.
keyPoints: FIELD: fenofibrate reduced first-laser need and slowed progression
ACCORD Eye: fenofibrate + simvastatin ~40% relative reduction in progression
The evidence-based adjunct is adding fenofibrate, not switching statins

items.title: Why fenofibrate's retinopathy benefit is largely lipid-independent — an important nuance
items.content: Here is the nuance the simple 'fenofibrate just lowers triglycerides' story misses: in both FIELD and ACCORD Eye the retinopathy benefit did NOT correlate well with the degree of lipid lowering and appeared largely independent of baseline or on-treatment triglyceride/LDL levels. That argues for direct, lipid-independent retinal actions of PPAR-α agonism — proposed mechanisms include downregulating intraretinal VEGF, preserving endothelial tight-junction proteins (e.g. claudin-5) and reducing apoptosis/inflammation in retinal capillaries — rather than benefit flowing purely from triglyceride reduction. The practical consequences are real: you may consider fenofibrate for retinopathy progression even when triglycerides are not markedly elevated, and you should not assume that simply normalising triglycerides by other means reproduces the trial effect. State this as the honest, slightly counter-intuitive truth rather than overselling the triglyceride mechanism.
keyPoints: Retinopathy benefit in FIELD/ACCORD was largely independent of lipid lowering
Implicates direct PPAR-α retinal effects (VEGF↓, tight-junction preservation), not just TG lowering
So benefit isn't guaranteed by normalising triglycerides through other routes

items.title: How triglycerides and ApoB relate to hard exudates — association, with care
items.content: Hard exudates are lipid (and lipoprotein) deposits that leak from incompetent retinal capillaries, and observational data (including ETDRS) link elevated serum lipids — including LDL and triglycerides — with more retinal hard exudates and worse visual outcomes. So controlling atherogenic dyslipidaemia, including raised triglycerides and ApoB, is a reasonable adjunct, and his triglycerides of 220 mg/dL are above the ~150 mg/dL threshold that prompts attention. But be precise: this is an association, and lowering triglycerides has not, by itself, been proven to clear established exudates the way the fenofibrate trials acted on progression/laser endpoints. Treat lipid optimisation as a sensible long-term modifier of exudate burden, not a guaranteed exudate-clearing therapy.
keyPoints: Hard exudates are lipoprotein deposits; serum lipids (LDL and TG) associate with them
TG >150 mg/dL warrants attention; ApoB captures atherogenic particle burden
Association ≠ proof that TG lowering clears established exudates

items.title: How to add fenofibrate safely — renal dosing and the gemfibrozil trap
items.content: Adding a fibrate to a statin needs three safety checks. (1) Renal function: fenofibrate is renally cleared, causes a reversible rise in serum creatinine, and is dose-reduced in moderate chronic kidney disease and avoided in severe impairment — so check eGFR before and during therapy and do not start it at low eGFR. (2) Choice of fibrate: combine a statin with fenofibrate, never gemfibrozil — gemfibrozil inhibits statin glucuronidation and sharply raises myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk, whereas fenofibrate is the safe partner. (3) Monitoring: baseline and periodic liver function and creatinine, counsel on muscle symptoms, and review interacting drugs. Typical dosing is fenofibrate ~145–160 mg daily (200 mg micronised in FIELD). These caveats are why fenofibrate is an adjunct added thoughtfully, not a reflex add-on.
keyPoints: Fenofibrate: reversible creatinine rise; dose-reduce in moderate CKD, avoid in severe
Pair statins with fenofibrate, NEVER gemfibrozil (myopathy/rhabdomyolysis)
Check eGFR/LFTs, counsel on muscle symptoms; ~145–160 mg daily

items.title: Why 'fenofibrate for the retina' must be flagged as off-label and region-dependent
items.content: Be honest about regulatory status: using fenofibrate specifically to slow diabetic retinopathy is off-label in many jurisdictions, even though the trial evidence is good and some regulators (e.g. Australia's TGA) have approved it to reduce progression of diabetic retinopathy in type 2 diabetes with existing retinopathy. Guidelines increasingly mention it as an option, but practice varies by country and payer. The teaching discipline is to present this as evidence-supported but not universally licensed for the eye, to document the shared decision and the cardiometabolic rationale, and to coordinate with ophthalmology rather than implying it is a standard, approved DME treatment everywhere. Flagging the off-label/region-dependent status is part of consenting the patient properly.
keyPoints: Retinopathy is an off-label fenofibrate use in many regions (approved in some, e.g. TGA)
Evidence-supported but not universally licensed for the eye — varies by country/payer
Document shared decision and coordinate with ophthalmology

items.title: Why lipid therapy is adjunctive — and what actually treats sight-threatening DME
items.content: Keep the hierarchy straight. Acute, centre-involving, vision-threatening diabetic macular edema is managed primarily by ophthalmology with intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy (first-line for centre-involving DME) or focal/grid laser, and sometimes intravitreal steroids — these are the interventions that protect vision now. Lipid management (statin to target ± fenofibrate) is a long-term adjunctive modifier that slows progression and may reduce exudate burden over time; it does not replace timely ophthalmic treatment, and prioritising a statin switch over an anti-VEGF referral would be a serious error. Underpinning all of it is the foundation that drives DME most powerfully: tight glycaemic control and blood-pressure control. So the order is glycaemia/BP and ophthalmology first, lipids as the adjunct.
keyPoints: Centre-involving/vision-threatening DME: anti-VEGF (first-line) or focal laser — primary therapy
Lipids (statin ± fenofibrate) are adjunctive, not a substitute for ophthalmic treatment
Foundation is glycaemic and blood-pressure control

items.title: How to manage lipids in diabetic dyslipidaemia with retinopathy — a one-page rule
items.content: Framework for this patient: (1) Refer/continue ophthalmology — centre-involving DME needs anti-VEGF or focal laser now; lipids do not defer that. (2) Optimise glycaemic and blood-pressure control — the strongest modifiable drivers of DME. (3) Hit the systemic lipid target — high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 or rosuvastatin 20–40) to LDL-C <55–70 mg/dL per risk, adding ezetimibe ± PCSK9i as needed; choose the molecule on potency/tolerability/interactions, not the eye, so a switch is reasonable only if it helps reach target or reduces side-effects. (4) Consider adding fenofibrate to slow retinopathy progression/laser need (ACCORD Eye, FIELD), after checking eGFR (renal dosing; avoid in severe CKD), pairing only with a statin (never gemfibrozil), and flagging the off-label/region-dependent status — recognising the benefit is largely lipid-independent. (5) Monitor LFTs, creatinine and muscle symptoms; document the shared decision.
keyPoints: Ophthalmology (anti-VEGF/laser) + glycaemia/BP first
Statin to systemic target (molecule by potency/tolerability/interactions), ± ezetimibe/PCSK9i
Add fenofibrate for retinopathy (check eGFR, statin-only pairing, off-label) — benefit largely lipid-independent

items.question: Why should you not switch this patient from atorvastatin to rosuvastatin to treat his macular edema?
items.answer: Because there is no high-quality head-to-head evidence that either statin is superior for diabetic macular edema or hard exudates. Any retinal benefit of statins is a class effect operating through systemic LDL-C/ApoB lowering and anti-inflammatory stabilisation of the blood–retinal barrier — not a molecule-specific property. The lipophilicity-versus-hydrophilicity argument (atorvastatin crossing membranes more readily, with hypothesised intraretinal pleiotropic effects) is mechanistically interesting but unproven clinically, and rosuvastatin's greater potency delivers an equivalent systemic effect anyway. So the choice between them should be governed by reaching the systemic LDL-C/ApoB target with the best tolerability and fewest drug interactions, and a switch is justified only if it helps achieve that — not as a treatment aimed at the eye. The evidence-based pharmacological lever for the retina is adding fenofibrate, not swapping statins.
keyPoints: No molecule-specific evidence for DME — statin benefit is class-level/systemic
Lipophilicity advantage is an unproven hypothesis; rosuvastatin's potency offsets it
Choose by systemic target/tolerability; the real retinal adjunct is fenofibrate

items.question: How would you manage lipids in this man with worsening DME and hard exudates?
items.answer: Order the actions by impact. (1) Ensure ophthalmology review — centre-involving/vision-threatening DME is treated primarily with anti-VEGF (first-line) or focal laser; lipid therapy does not defer this. (2) Optimise glycaemic and blood-pressure control, the strongest modifiable drivers of DME. (3) Drive the statin to the systemic target (LDL-C <55–70 mg/dL by cardiovascular risk) using high-intensity dosing ± ezetimibe/PCSK9i, choosing the molecule on potency, tolerability and interactions rather than on the retina — so a switch to rosuvastatin is reasonable only if it helps reach target or improves tolerability. (4) Consider adding fenofibrate to slow retinopathy progression and reduce first-laser need (ACCORD Eye, FIELD), after checking eGFR (renal dosing; avoid in severe CKD), pairing it only with a statin (never gemfibrozil), monitoring LFTs/creatinine/muscle symptoms, and flagging that this is an off-label, region-dependent indication whose benefit is largely lipid-independent. (5) Document the shared decision.
keyPoints: Ophthalmology (anti-VEGF/laser) + glycaemia/BP are primary
Statin to systemic target by potency/tolerability; add ezetimibe/PCSK9i if needed
Add fenofibrate (eGFR check, statin-only pairing, off-label) for retinopathy

items.stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, DME and prominent macular hard exudates is on atorvastatin 20 mg with near-target LDL-C. The team asks whether to switch to rosuvastatin to treat the eye. What is the best advice?
options: Do not switch for the eye — no statin is proven superior for DME; choose statins by systemic target, tolerability and interactions
Switch to rosuvastatin, which is proven superior for macular edema
Stop the statin, as statins worsen diabetic retinopathy
Switch to a lipophilic statin because lipophilicity is proven to clear hard exudates
items.explanation: There is no head-to-head evidence that one statin is superior for DME or hard exudates; the benefit is a class effect via systemic lipid/anti-inflammatory action. Statin choice should be driven by reaching the LDL-C/ApoB target with the best tolerability and fewest interactions. Statins are not stopped, and the lipophilicity advantage is unproven.

items.stem: Which intervention has the best trial evidence for slowing diabetic retinopathy progression and reducing first laser treatment?
options: Adding fenofibrate to statin therapy (ACCORD Eye, FIELD)
Switching atorvastatin to rosuvastatin
Doubling the statin dose alone
Adding a high-dose fish-oil supplement
items.explanation: FIELD showed fenofibrate reduced first-laser need and slowed progression, and ACCORD Eye showed fenofibrate added to simvastatin reduced retinopathy progression by ~40% over 4 years. A statin swap, dose doubling alone, or fish oil do not have comparable retinopathy-endpoint evidence.

items.stem: Which statement about fenofibrate's retinopathy benefit in ACCORD Eye and FIELD is most accurate?
options: The benefit was largely independent of the degree of lipid/triglyceride lowering, implicating direct retinal PPAR-α effects
The benefit was entirely explained by the magnitude of triglyceride reduction
The benefit only occurred in patients with normal triglycerides
Fenofibrate worsened retinopathy in both trials
items.explanation: In both trials the retinopathy benefit correlated poorly with lipid lowering and appeared largely lipid-independent, pointing to direct PPAR-α retinal actions (VEGF downregulation, tight-junction preservation). It was not simply a triglyceride-lowering effect, was not restricted to normal-triglyceride patients, and fenofibrate did not worsen retinopathy.

items.stem: Which fibrate should NOT be combined with a statin because of markedly increased myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk?
options: Gemfibrozil
Fenofibrate
Bezafibrate at standard dose
Any fibrate is equally safe with a statin
items.explanation: Gemfibrozil inhibits statin glucuronidation, raising statin levels and the risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, so it should not be combined with a statin. Fenofibrate is the preferred fibrate to pair with a statin. The fibrates are not equally safe in combination.

items.stem: Before adding fenofibrate to this patient's statin, which test most directly determines whether it is safe to start?
options: eGFR / renal function (fenofibrate is renally cleared, causes a reversible creatinine rise, and is contraindicated in severe impairment)
Serum TSH
Fasting insulin level
Vitamin B12
items.explanation: Fenofibrate is renally cleared, causes a reversible rise in creatinine, requires dose reduction in moderate CKD and is avoided in severe renal impairment — so eGFR is the key gating test. TSH, fasting insulin and B12 do not determine fenofibrate safety.

items.stem: This patient has centre-involving, vision-threatening DME. What is the primary treatment?
options: Ophthalmic intravitreal anti-VEGF (or focal/grid laser) — lipid therapy is adjunctive
Switching the statin, which is the definitive treatment for centre-involving DME
Fenofibrate monotherapy instead of ophthalmic treatment
Observation, since lipid control will resolve it
items.explanation: Centre-involving, vision-threatening DME is treated primarily with intravitreal anti-VEGF (first-line) or focal/grid laser by ophthalmology. Lipid management (statin to target ± fenofibrate) and glycaemic/BP control are adjunctive, long-term modifiers and must not delay sight-saving ophthalmic treatment.

items.statement: Head-to-head trials have shown rosuvastatin is superior to atorvastatin for treating diabetic macular edema.
items.explanation: False. There is no high-quality head-to-head evidence of one statin being superior for DME or hard exudates; any statin benefit is a class effect. Statin choice should be driven by the systemic lipid target, tolerability and drug interactions.

items.statement: Lipid management can substitute for anti-VEGF or focal laser in acute, vision-threatening diabetic macular edema.
items.explanation: False. Lipid therapy is an adjunctive, long-term modifier. Acute, centre-involving, vision-threatening DME is treated primarily with intravitreal anti-VEGF or focal laser; delaying these for a lipid intervention risks vision.

items.assertion: Fenofibrate can be considered to slow diabetic retinopathy progression even when triglycerides are only modestly elevated.
items.reason: In ACCORD Eye and FIELD, fenofibrate's retinopathy benefit was largely independent of the degree of lipid lowering.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the retinopathy benefit in the trials correlated poorly with lipid lowering and appeared largely lipid-independent (implicating direct retinal PPAR-α effects), fenofibrate may be considered for retinopathy progression even without marked hypertriglyceridaemia. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: A patient with diabetic macular edema should be switched to the most lipophilic statin to maximise intraretinal pleiotropic effects.
items.reason: Lipophilic statins have been proven in clinical trials to clear macular hard exudates better than hydrophilic statins.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. There is no proven clinical superiority of lipophilic over hydrophilic statins for clearing hard exudates or treating DME; the lipophilicity/pleiotropy idea is an unproven hypothesis, and statin choice should be driven by the systemic lipid target and tolerability. Hence option 4.

items.question: What should actually drive the choice between atorvastatin and rosuvastatin in this patient?
items.answer: The systemic agenda, not the eye: pick the statin (and dose) that reaches the patient's cardiovascular LDL-C/ApoB target — broadly LDL-C <55–70 mg/dL depending on risk — with the best tolerability and the fewest drug–drug interactions. Because there is no proven molecule-specific advantage for diabetic macular edema, a switch is justified only if it helps achieve the lipid target or reduces side-effects, not as a treatment aimed at the retina. The evidence-based pharmacological lever for the retina is adding fenofibrate, not swapping statins.

items.question: Summarise the ACCORD Eye and FIELD evidence for fenofibrate and the key mechanistic nuance.
items.answer: FIELD (type 2 diabetes) showed fenofibrate reduced the need for first laser treatment for retinopathy and slowed progression in those with existing retinopathy. ACCORD Eye showed fenofibrate added to simvastatin reduced diabetic retinopathy progression by roughly 40% over four years. The key nuance is that in both trials the retinopathy benefit was largely independent of the degree of lipid/triglyceride lowering, implicating direct retinal PPAR-α effects (downregulating VEGF, preserving endothelial tight-junction proteins such as claudin-5) rather than benefit flowing purely from triglyceride reduction.

items.question: List the practical caveats before adding fenofibrate for retinopathy.
items.answer: (1) Renal function: fenofibrate is renally cleared, causes a reversible creatinine rise, is dose-reduced in moderate CKD and avoided in severe impairment — check eGFR before and during therapy. (2) Fibrate choice: combine only with a statin and never use gemfibrozil with a statin (markedly increased myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk); fenofibrate is the safe partner. (3) Monitoring: baseline and periodic LFTs and creatinine, and counsel on muscle symptoms. (4) Governance: using fenofibrate specifically for retinopathy is off-label in many jurisdictions (approved in some, e.g. Australia's TGA), so document the shared decision and coordinate with ophthalmology. Typical dose is ~145–160 mg daily (200 mg micronised in FIELD).

items.stem: Why should switching from atorvastatin to rosuvastatin not be recommended for treating diabetic macular edema?
options: A) Rosuvastatin has greater lipophilicity, allowing better intraretinal penetration
B) Atorvastatin is more potent in lowering LDL-C than rosuvastatin
C) The retinal benefits of statins are class-specific (via systemic LDL-C/ApoB lowering) rather than molecule-specific
D) Rosuvastatin has fewer drug interactions compared to atorvastatin
items.explanation: Statin benefits for diabetic macular edema are attributed to systemic LDL-C/ApoB lowering and anti-inflammatory effects, not molecule-specific properties. Switching statins does not improve retinal outcomes unless it aids in achieving systemic lipid targets. Fenofibrate, not statin switching, is the evidence-based adjunct for retinal protection.

items.stem: In a man with worsening diabetic macular edema (DME) and hard exudates, which sequence best reflects the priority of lipid management steps?
options: A) Initiate high-intensity statin therapy immediately, then refer to ophthalmology
B) Optimize glucose/BP control, then refer to ophthalmology for anti-VEGF/laser, followed by statin escalation and fenofibrate if needed
C) Refer to ophthalmology first, then optimize glucose/BP, followed by statin escalation and fenofibrate if needed
D) Start fenofibrate with eGFR monitoring, then refer to ophthalmology and initiate statin therapy
items.explanation: Ophthalmology review and metabolic control (glucose/BP) are primary for vision-threatening DME. Statin therapy should target LDL-C with high-intensity agents, and fenofibrate (if used) is adjunctive, requiring eGFR checks and statin pairing.

items.stem: What should primarily drive the choice between atorvastatin and rosuvastatin in a patient with diabetic macular edema?
options: A) Achieving LDL-C targets and minimizing drug–drug interactions
B) Targeting diabetic macular edema specifically
C) Patient preference for generic medications
D) Cost-effectiveness of the statin
items.explanation: The choice should prioritize achieving cardiovascular LDL-C/ApoB targets with optimal tolerability and minimal drug interactions, as there is no proven molecule-specific benefit for diabetic macular edema. Switching statins is only justified if it improves lipid control or reduces side effects, not for retinal outcomes.

items.assertion: Fenofibrate reduces progression of diabetic retinopathy independently of lipid/lowering effects.
items.reason: This is due to direct activation of retinal PPAR-α, which downregulates VEGF and preserves endothelial tight-junction proteins.
items.explanation: Both ACCORD Eye and FIELD trials demonstrated retinopathy benefits not fully explained by triglyceride reduction, supporting PPAR-α-mediated mechanisms as the primary pathway.
